title: What Is Dividend Yield? Definition, Formula & Examples
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: What is Dividend Yield? Learn the definition, formula, and real-world examples of Dividend Yield. Our comprehensive guide explains how Dividend Yield works and why it matters for investors.
excerpt: Dividend yield measures the annual dividend income relative to the stock price, expressed as a percentage. It tells investors how much cash return they can expect from dividends alone, separate from a...

content.definition: Dividend yield measures the annual dividend income relative to the stock price, expressed as a percentage. It tells investors how much cash return they can expect from dividends alone, separate from any stock price appreciation. A stock priced at $100 paying $3 in annual dividends has a 3% dividend yield. This metric is particularly important for income-focused investors and retirees who rely on portfolio cash flow.
content.formula: Dividend Yield = Annual Dividends Per Share / Share Price × 100%
content.example: Coca-Cola trades at $60/share and pays $1.84 annually in dividends. Its dividend yield is 3.07% ($1.84 / $60 × 100). If you invest $10,000 in KO, you would receive approximately $307 per year in dividend income.
content.types: High-yield stocks (4%+) often come from utilities, REITs, and MLPs but may signal financial stress. Moderate-yield stocks (2-4%) from established companies offer income plus growth potential. Low-yield or zero-yield growth stocks reinvest earnings for expansion rather than distributing cash.
content.whyItMatters: Understanding Dividend Yield is essential for making informed investment decisions. Whether you are evaluating individual stocks, planning your portfolio allocation, or comparing investment options, Dividend Yield provides a quantitative framework that removes guesswork and helps you assess value objectively. Professional investors, analysts, and financial advisors all rely on Dividend Yield as a core metric in their decision-making process.

image.alt: Scrabble tiles forming the word 'YIELD' on a marble surface, symbolizing finance and investment.
